.raw/InnBlockchain/EU Compliance/mar-che.notes: First MAR (Reg (EU) No 596/2014) concept page added to the wiki. Tenth EU compliance checklist ingest in the 2026-05-24–25 cluster (after MiCA v2.3, DORA v1.2, MiFID II v1.1, DLT Pilot v1.2, GDPR v1.1, TFR v1.0, IPR v1.0, EU Listing Act v1.0, CCD2 v1.0). MAR is the ongoing-disclosure + market-conduct counterpart to the Prospectus Regulation's offer-time disclosure — pairs explicitly with [[Prospectus-Regulation]] (offer-time) and [[EU-Listing-Act]] (the horizontal amender that materially changed MAR Art 17 / 19 / 30 from 4 Dec 2024). Structural framing on concept page: MAR scope is instrument-based (Art 2), not entity-based — the single most common scoping mistake. Once a financial instrument is admitted or pending admission to an EU RM/MTF/OTF/SMEGM, MAR applies regardless of issuer authorisation status. Concept page leads with the three substantive prohibitions (Arts 14, 15: insider dealing, unlawful disclosure, market manipulation), three substantive ongoing-disclosure obligations on issuers (Arts 17, 18, 19), and two safe-harbour exemptions (Arts 5, 6). Listing Act amendments table is the most operationally-loaded section because every already-listed issuer must re-baseline the disclosure-committee playbook (Art 17(1) final-event-only + new Art 17(1a) intermediate-step confidentiality), PDMR policy (€5k → €20k annual aggregate; MS opt-up €50k), buy-back reporting routing (single 'most relevant market' NCA per MiFIR Art 26), and sanctions exposure (2% turnover / €2.5M floor + €1M SME alternative for Art 17; 0.8% / €1M / €400k SME for Arts 18-19). Companion checklist .raw/InnBlockchain/EU Compliance/mar-checklist.md v1.0 covers all 39 Articles + Annex I indicators across 12 sections + 2 appendices: Section 1 scope / 2 buy-back + stabilisation + public-policy exemptions / 3 inside-information definition with protracted-process analysis / 4 market soundings / 5 manipulation incl Annex I indicators / 6 issuer ongoing-disclosure (Arts 16 STOR, 17 disclosure, 18 insider lists, 19 PDMR) — THE Listing Act section / 7 investment recommendations + media / 8 competent authority cooperation incl new Arts 25a/25b/29 / 9 administrative sanctions Art 30 + Art 31-34 / 10 tokenised instruments (on-chain surveillance, insider-list challenges, PDMR wallet monitoring) / 11 first-meeting documentation checklist / 12 red flags + Appendix A legal references + Appendix B delta-only summary for already-listed issuers. Verification methodology: tenth use this week of the local-EUR-Lex-HTML pattern. User had previously saved MAR.html (561KB local copy of EUR-Lex page for Reg (EU) No 596/2014). Verbatim citations cross-checked: document title verbatim ('REGULATION (EU) No 596/2014 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL of 16 April 2014 on market abuse (market abuse regulation) and repealing Directive 2003/6/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council and Commission Directives 2003/124/EC, 2003/125/EC and 2004/72/EC'); Article structure 1-39 confirmed (Subject matter / Scope / Definitions / Notifications and list of financial instruments / Exemption for buy-back programmes and stabilisation / Exemption for monetary and public debt management activities and climate policy activities / Inside information / Insider dealing / Legitimate behaviour / Unlawful disclosure of inside information / Market soundings / Market manipulation / Accepted market practices / Prohibition of insider dealing and of unlawful disclosure of inside information / Prohibition of market manipulation / Prevention and detection of market abuse / Public disclosure of inside information / Insider lists / Managers' transactions / Investment recommendations and statistics / Disclosure or dissemination of information in the media / Competent authorities / Powers of competent authorities / Cooperation with ESMA / Obligation to cooperate / Cooperation with third countries / Professional secrecy / Data protection / Disclosure of personal data to third countries / Administrative sanctions and other administrative measures / Exercise of supervisory powers and imposition of sanctions / Reporting of infringements / Exchange of information with ESMA / Publication of decisions / Exercise of the delegation / Committee procedure / Repeal of Directive 2003/6/EC and its implementing measures / Report / Entry into force and application); Annex I market-manipulation indicators referenced for surveillance build. Listing Act deltas folded in throughout (not as a separate section but inline at each affected Article) because the post-Listing-Act regime IS the operative baseline as of 2026-05-25. Companion-instrument boundary callouts: (a) MAR vs MiCA Title VI — both contain similar prohibitions but different regimes; classification gate determines which applies; explicit warning callout in concept page; (b) MAR vs CRIM-MAD (Dir 2014/57/EU) — parallel criminal-sanctions regime, Member-State-transposed, not covered horizontally because variation across MS is too wide; gap callout flagged in concept page. Key insights: (1) Scope is instrument-based, not entity-based — non-EU issuer with EU-DLT-MTF-admitted token is in MAR scope; pre-listing issuer with filed admission request is in MAR scope; single most common scoping mistake. (2) Art 17 protracted-process narrowing is the highest-impact change in MAR history — every disclosure-committee playbook in the EU needs rewriting as of 4 Dec 2024. (3) PDMR €20k threshold is annual aggregate not per-transaction — common aggregation engine bug. (4) Buy-back reporting now to single NCA (most relevant market under MiFIR Art 26) — common routing mis-update. (5) Tokenised securities surveillance under Art 16 must cover wash trading, validator/sequencer front-running, oracle manipulation, sandwich attacks — Art 16 venue duty + Art 16(2) PPAET duty. (6) Insider list per-event sections required (not single rolling list) per ESMA Q&A — common technical breach. (7) PDMR closed period is 30 calendar days not trading days. Methodology pattern: tenth use this week of the local-EUR-Lex-HTML verification pattern. Workflow is now identical ten-for-ten (mica.html / dora.html / dlt.html / mifid2.html + mifid3-791.html / amla.html / gdpr.html / tfr.html / IPR.html / eu-listing.html / ccd2.html / MAR.html). Cross-link updates: [[Prospectus-Regulation]] + [[EU-Listing-Act]] + [[MiFID-II]] + [[MiFIR-Operational-Mechanics]] frontmatter related: blocks all get MAR-Regulation added. [[EU-Fintech-Compliance-Landscape]] now references MAR substantively in three places: (a) new MAR row in top regulation table with wikilink to MAR-Regulation; (b) new MAR block in CONDITIONAL Dependency Map; (c) new MAR entry in Primary Sources / Legislation in force table with EUR-Lex ELI URL https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2014/596/oj. NOT created this ingest: MAR-Operational-Mechanics practitioner companion (gap callout in concept page) — deferred until ESMA Q&A / Delegated Reg 2016/522 indicators / Delegated Reg 2016/1052 buy-back conditions / Implementing Reg 2016/347 insider-list format / Implementing Reg 2016/523 PDMR notification template can be extracted from operational engagements. Self-pushback recorded: prior turn (eu-listing ingest) I argued MAR was 'completeness, not service-relevance' for InnBlockchain's actual ICPs (Crypto Native Seg 1 pre-listing, FinTech ICP WealthTech already-licensed, Crypto Products $5k-$20k, Channel Partner routing). User instructed me to create the checklist anyway. The framing has shifted to: MAR is a reference body, not active sales surface; MAR-aware smart-contract architecture documents (upgrade governance, oracle insulation, multisig key-holder identification for insider-list inclusion) are the InnBlockchain-relevant deliverable shape, not standalone MAR advisory.

.raw/InnBlockchain/EU Compliance/ccd2-ch.notes: First CCD2 (Consumer Credit Directive 2 — Directive (EU) 2023/2225) concept page added to the wiki — ninth EU compliance checklist ingest in the 2026-05-24–25 cluster (after MiCA v2.3, DORA v1.2, MiFID II v1.1, DLT Pilot v1.2, GDPR v1.1, TFR v1.0, IPR v1.0, EU Listing Act v1.0). Structural delta: CCD2 is the FIRST consumer-protection directive added to the wiki (every prior compliance ingest was a financial-services regulation or supervisor-architecture instrument scoped to FI/CASP/PSP/PI/EMI entity types). CCD2 cuts horizontally across the FinTech ICP at LendTech AND leaks into Crypto Native segments wherever a consumer-fiat-credit relationship exists (embedded BNPL, stablecoin-denominated credit-lines, NFT marketplace pay-later, crypto-collateralised fiat loans). CCD2 is also the FIRST maximum-harmonisation directive — distinct from GDPR/MiCA/DORA/AMLR/IPR/Listing-Act (all regulations with direct effect): national transposing law is operative, ~17 explicit Member-State regulatory choices create up to 27 overlays, no harmonised EU passport for non-bank creditors (Art 37). Concept page leads with maximum-harmonisation-directive-not-regulation distinction, then CCD1→CCD2 scope-expansion comparison table (BNPL captured under Art 2(2)(h), sub-€200 captured under Art 2(8)(a), free-of-interest captured under Art 2(8)(b), leasing-with-option-to-buy captured under Art 2(2)(g) exclusion narrowing), then lifecycle obligation spine table, then Art 18(8) 'stronger than GDPR Art 22' callout, then Art 18(3)+19(5) social-network-data ban, then Member-State-set penalty regime as inverse of MiCA/DORA/GDPR, then key dates table with Art 47/48/49 milestones, then CCD2 ↔ GDPR / AI Act / AMLR / DORA / NPL Directive / MiCA interaction table. No new source page — existing InnBlockchain-EU-Compliance-Overview-2026 (created 2026-04-16) already covers CCD2 in the nine-key-regulations table (verification-artefact-only, same pattern as IPR / DLT Pilot / EU Listing Act). Verification methodology: ninth use this week of the local-EUR-Lex-HTML pattern (live EUR-Lex blocked WebFetch/curl with HTTP 202 anti-bot wall — same blocker as every EU compliance ingest this week). User had previously saved ccd2.html (~9000 lines from EUR-Lex eli/dir/2023/2225/oj/eng). Read targeted Article ranges in-context rather than full HTMLParser strip: Arts 1–3 / 7–13 / 14–17 / 18–19 / 26–29 / 32–36 / 37–38 / 41 / 44 / 47–49. Borrowing money costs money' mandatory wording; Art 8(3) Standard Information six elements; Art 8(7) three absolute advertising prohibitions; Art 8(8) three optional advertising prohibitions; Art 10(1) SECCI good-time + late-disclosure 1-day reminder window; Art 10(3) SECCI 12-element one-page first part; Art 10(5) SECCI 17-element second part; Art 14(1) tying prohibition; Art 14(4) oncology right-to-be-forgotten max 15 years post-treatment; Art 14(5) 3-day insurance comparison window; Art 15 inferred-consent ban with default-options/pre-ticked-boxes verbatim; Art 17 unsolicited credit ban verbatim; Art 18(3) creditworthiness data restrictions including special-category-data + social-networks exclusion verbatim; Art 18(7) creditor cannot cancel for incorrect assessment; Art 18(8) right-to-human-intervention three-element list (explanation + own POV + review); Art 18(10) re-assessment on significant credit-limit increase; Art 19(5) database special-category + social-networks exclusion; Art 19(7) 30-day arrears-registration notification; Art 26(1) 14-day withdrawal; Art 26(2) 12-months-and-14-days extension if Art 21(1)(p) info missing; Art 26(5) 30-day repayment-after-withdrawal; Art 29(2) compensation caps 1% / 0.5%; Art 29(4) Member-State threshold ≤€10,000 in 12 months; Art 31 Member-State rate-cap mandate + Art 31(3) Commission publication by 20 Nov 2027 + Art 31(4) EBA report by 20 Nov 2029; Art 32 conduct of business; Art 33 staff knowledge & competence; Art 35 forbearance toolkit eight-item list (refinancing, term extension, type change, payment deferral, rate reduction, payment holiday, partial repayments, currency conversion, partial forgiveness + debt consolidation); Art 36(2) early-detection of financial difficulty + Art 36(4) first MS reporting deadline 20 Nov 2026 + Commission report 20 Nov 2028; Art 37(1) admission + registration + supervision by independent competent authority + Art 37(2) CI/PI/EMI exemptions + Art 37(3) SME ancillary intermediary Member-State exemption; Art 38(a)–(d) credit intermediary four-item disclosure list; Art 41(5) Member-State notification deadline 20 Nov 2025; Art 42(1) maximum harmonisation + Art 42(2) ~17 Member-State regulatory choices list verbatim; Art 43 imperative nature + non-circumvention; Art 44(1) effective/proportionate/dissuasive + Member-State notification by 20 Nov 2026 + no EU ceiling; Art 44(3) public-disclosure power; Art 47 CCD1 repealed 20 Nov 2026 + grandfathering of existing agreements + carve-ins for open-end (Arts 23, 24, 25(1) 2nd sentence, 25(2), 28, 39); Art 48(1) transposition 20 Nov 2025 + application 20 Nov 2026; Art 49 entry into force 20 days post-publication = 19 Nov 2023. Stress-test of three pressure points flagged to user pre-mirror: (1) BNPL framing confidence — Art 2(2)(h) carve-out is structurally complex; competent BNPL operator can still argue exception applies for sub-50-day interest-free non-third-party-financed payment terms; checklist asserts in-scope confidently in places where it deserves more hedging; (2) Art 18(8) reading vs unsettled NCA guidance — concept page asserts Art 18(8) is unconditional (no consent/contract-necessity gateway like GDPR Art 22); this is best-current-reading of the text but has not been pressure-tested against EDPB/EBA Q&A (none exists pre-application-date); (3) Crypto-lending scope analysis is positioning rather than settled law — CCD2 doesn't name crypto once; analysis depends on whether a 'credit agreement' with a 'consumer' and an identifiable 'creditor' actually exists at the protocol layer (often it doesn't — pure protocol over-collateralised loans lack a counterparty creditor). User proceeded with mirror; concept page Section 12 retains the explicit 'decision rests on whether...' hedge. Key insights flagged on concept page: (1) BNPL is the structural scope shift, not a marginal extension — CCD1 Art 2(2)(f) excluded most deferred-payment products; CCD2 Art 2(2)(h) closes that gap with a deliberately narrow carve-out; large-platform information-society service providers face a tighter 14-day window; (2) Art 18(8) is stronger than GDPR Art 22 — no contract-necessity / consent gateway; ML-driven underwriting stacks built on GDPR Art 22 contract-necessity defence lose that defence under CCD2 from 20 Nov 2026; deepest CCD2 ↔ GDPR ↔ AI-Act intersection and primary InnBlockchain cross-sell hook into AI-Act audit work; (3) Art 18(3)+19(5) social-network-data ban prohibits a popular 2018–2023 alt-data thin-file underwriting architecture; CCD2 is also STRICTER than GDPR on special-category data (no Art 9(2) condition can override); (4) Penalty regime is inverse of MiCA/DORA/GDPR — no EU ceiling, Member-State-set, wide divergence (DE criminal pathway StGB §§264a-266, IT/FR/NL admin fines into millions); Art 44(3) public-disclosure power likely more consequential lever for brand-led non-bank lenders; (5) No harmonised passport for non-bank creditors — Art 37 admission/registration is Member-State-by-Member-State (subject to Art 37(2) exemptions for already-passported CI/PI/EMI); pan-EU non-bank consumer-credit play means up to 27 supervisory relationships; (6) Maximum-harmonisation with ~17 Member-State regulatory choices is the operational headache — national transposing law is operative, CCD2 itself is the floor; (7) CCD2's crypto exposure is narrow but real — technology-neutral material scope captures fiat-denominated credit regardless of underlying technology; greatest exposure is embedded-finance + BNPL crypto-stack intersection. Open items / known gaps preserved on concept page: (a) National transposing laws per Member State — outside Level 1 scope; (b) EBA report on rate-cap implementation due 20 Nov 2029 (3.5 years out); (c) Commission delegated acts on Annex III APR-calculation assumptions (Art 30(5)) and Annex I SECCI form updates may emerge through 2026–2027; (d) Pure-DeFi-protocol consumer-lending scope is contested and may need ESMA/EBA Q&A before settled. Methodology pattern is now nine-for-nine (mica.html / dora.html / dlt.html / mifid2.html + mifid3-791.html / amla.html / gdpr.html / tfr.html / IPR.html / eu-listing.html / ccd2.html).

.raw/InnBlockchain/EU Compliance/eu-list.notes: First EU Listing Act (Reg (EU) 2024/2809) concept page added to the wiki — eighth EU compliance checklist ingest in the 2026-05-24–25 cluster (after MiCA v2.3, DORA v1.2, MiFID II v1.1, DLT Pilot v1.2, GDPR v1.1, TFR v1.0, IPR v1.0). Structural delta vs every prior ingest: the EU Listing Act is the SECOND amending-act-only regime in the wiki (after IPR), but it is the FIRST that amends THREE host regulations in one instrument (Prospectus Reg 2017/1129 via Article 1, MAR Reg 596/2014 via Article 2, MiFIR Reg 600/2014 via Article 3) PLUS it travels in a package with two companion directives (Dir 2024/2810 amending MiFID II + repealing the 2001 Listing Directive 2001/34/EC; Dir 2024/2811 multi-vote shares on SMEGMs). Concept page accordingly leads with the 'three things the Listing Act does at Regulation level + two at Directive level' framing, then the problem statement (declining EU IPO flow, SME delistings), then the three-cliff calendar (4 Dec 2024 / 5 Mar 2026 / 5 Jun 2026) + companion-directive transposition cliffs (5 Jun 2026 / 5 Dec 2026), then the reform-limb map (who cares about which limb), then the navigation table to amended-host pages. Concept page kept DELIBERATELY thin on substantive Prospectus / MiFIR / MiFID II content because those have dedicated concept pages already (Prospectus-Regulation.md / MiFIR-Operational-Mechanics.md / MiFID-II.md / MiFID-II-NCA-Authorization-Mechanics.md); concept page acts as a horizontal index / package layer pointing readers to the relevant amended-host concept page + the corresponding .raw practitioner checklist. Verification methodology: eighth use this week of the local-EUR-Lex-HTML pattern. User had previously saved eu-listing.html (677KB local copy of EUR-Lex page for Reg (EU) 2024/2809). Verbatim citations cross-checked: document title (REGULATION (EU) 2024/2809 OF 23 October 2024 amending Regulations (EU) 2017/1129, (EU) No 596/2014 and (EU) No 600/2014); Article 1 amendments to Prospectus Regulation including new Article 14a (EU Follow-on prospectus), new Article 15a (EU Growth issuance prospectus), Article 29 (Equivalence) and Article 30 (Cooperation with third countries) replacements, Article 48a (Transitional provisions); Article 2 amendments to MAR including new SI definition in Art 3, Art 5(3) buy-back single-NCA reporting, Art 17(1)+(1a) protracted-process narrowing to final event only, Art 19 PDMR €5k→€20k threshold (MS opt-up to €50k) implied via Art 30 administrative-sanction recalibration (2% turnover/€2.5M Art 17 / 0.8% turnover/€1M Art 18-19 / SME alternatives €1M and €400k), new Arts 25a (mechanism to exchange order data with significant-cross-border-dimension venues €100bn turnover + 50% cross-border ratio) and 25b (collaboration platforms), new Art 29 (personal data to third countries); Article 3 surgical MiFIR Article 25(2)(3) replacement (NOT a broad MiFIR rewrite — that lives in the separate MiFID/MiFIR Review Reg 2024/791); Article 4 application calendar — Art 4(1) default 4 Dec 2024, Art 4(2) 5 Mar 2026 for Art 1 point (7)(g) and points (11)–(14), Art 4(3) 5 Jun 2026 for Art 1 points (3), (6)(b)(c), (7)(a)–(f), 10(a)(i)(ii)(iii), 10(b)(c), 21(a) re Art 27(1) of PR, and Art 2 point (6)(a)(b)(c)(e), Art 4(4) MS deadline 5 Jun 2026 for MAR Art 2 point (14)(a), (15). Companion directives 2024/2810 (MiFID II + repeal of 2001/34/EC; transposition 5 Jun 2026) and 2024/2811 (multi-vote shares on SMEGMs; transposition 5 Dec 2026) covered in Section 6 of the checklist as 'companion' content — not in eu-listing.html itself, deliberately included for first-meeting completeness; flagged trade-off to user before mirroring. Key insights flagged in concept page: (1) The MAR Article 17 protracted-process narrowing to final event only is the single biggest ongoing-issuer impact — every already-listed issuer must rewrite its disclosure-committee playbook; (2) PDMR €5k→€20k threshold (MS opt-up €50k) is an annual aggregate, not per-transaction; (3) buy-back reporting routed to single 'most relevant market' NCA per Art 26 MiFIR, NOT to every venue's NCA — internal trade-reporting feed must change; (4) Multi-vote shares (Dir 2024/2811) apply ONLY at first admission to SMEGM, not retro-applicable to existing listed entities, and institutional investor / proxy-advisor / index-provider exclusion policies for dual-class structures are the demand-pool risk to model; (5) Critical to NOT confuse the Listing Act with the MiFID/MiFIR Review (Reg 2024/791 + Dir 2024/790) — both run in parallel, both touch MiFIR, both have 2026 application dates, but they are separate packages with separate scopes; the Listing Act amends MiFIR Article 25 only (record-keeping narrowing) whereas the MiFID/MiFIR Review substantively rewrites MiFIR transparency and reporting; (6) Tokenised transferable securities (security tokens, tokenised bonds, tokenised equity) sit INSIDE the Prospectus / MAR / MiFIR perimeter — and therefore inside the Listing Act perimeter — regardless of whether issued and traded on DLT infrastructure under the DLT Pilot Regime; the form does not change the substance; (7) The .raw checklist deliberately includes Sections 6.1/6.2 on companion directives 2024/2810 and 2024/2811 despite their not being in eu-listing.html — flagged the scope decision to the user with explicit option to cut, user said 'mirror' meaning keep them. Gap callouts preserved on concept page: (a) No dedicated MAR (Reg 596/2014) concept page yet — Listing Act's MAR Art 17/19/30 amendments are the biggest ongoing-issuer impact; Section 4 of the eu-listing checklist is the working reference; standalone MAR-Regulation concept page + MAR-Operational-Mechanics practitioner companion is the queued next-ingest candidate; (b) Listing Act Level 2 delegated acts under Reg 2024/2809 — particularly Annex restructuring for the new EU Follow-on and EU Growth issuance prospectus formats, the non-exhaustive list of 'final events' in protracted processes (MAR Art 17 delegated act), and the Member-State opt-up calibration for €50k PDMR threshold and €5M offer-threshold — still being adopted on a rolling basis through 2026. Methodology pattern is now eight-for-eight (mica.html / dora.html / dlt.html / mifid2.html + mifid3-791.html / amla.html / gdpr.html / tfr.html / IPR.html / eu-listing.html). verification_history.method: first-pass verbatim cross-check against locally-saved IPR.html
second-pass double-confirmation against IPR.html — 4 material corrections + 2 precision notes flagged
material_corrections: Section 8.3 — Art 11(1c) planned-maintenance carve-out conditions are OR-linked alternatives, not AND-cumulative as v1.0 bullet structure suggested. Source: 'due to planned maintenance where periods of non-availability are both foreseeable and short OR to a planned downtime of all instant credit transfers under the relevant payment scheme'. Restructured as (i)/(ii) with OR connector and common preconditions clearly separated.

Section 7.6 — Art 3(1) IPR amendment to PSD2 Art 10(1) reframed from 'tightened wording' (factually wrong) to 'scope extension'. The actual amendment (a) extends Article 10(1) directly to EMIs (previously the EMI safeguarding obligation reached PSD2 Art 10 indirectly via Article 7 of EMD2), and (b) expands the obligation-imposer from 'Member States' to 'Member States or competent authorities'. The substantive (a)/(b) safeguarding routes are materially unchanged — this is a structural amendment, not a tightening.

Section 5.1 + Section 10.1 critical-path callout — Art 5d obligation crystallises when SCT Inst offering begins, not unconditionally on 9 Jan 2025. Source Art 5d(1) binds 'PSPs offering instant credit transfers' — for PIs/EMIs whose own Art 5a deadline is 9 April 2027, Art 5d binds from offering go-live, not from 9 Jan 2025 in absolute terms. Inserted explicit scope-trigger callout above Section 5.1; reworded Section 10.1 critical-path note to distinguish euro-area CI deadline (9 Jan 2025) from PI/EMI offering-trigger.

Section 2.7 — packaged-orders rule covers 'number of payment orders' per package (count), not 'volume' (value). Source Art 5a(7) second subparagraph: 'PSPs shall not impose limits on the number of payment orders that can be submitted in a package of instant credit transfers which are lower than the limits they impose in respect of packages of other credit transfers.' v1.0 used 'per-package volume limits' which reads as aggregate-value caps — could mislead a reader into thinking IPR governs the total euro value, not the count of orders. Corrected to 'number-of-orders cap'.
precision_notes_flagged_not_changed: Section 2.6 — 'without difficulty and with immediate effect' is recital 19 language, not Article 5a(6) operative text. Body keeps the phrasing as a useful operational interpretation but flagged for source-grounding precision.

Section 8.2 — 'Senior management and management-body members can be personally penalised' is supported by recital 27 (last sentence) rather than operative Article 11 text. Body keeps the statement as a recital-derived implication.

.raw/InnBlockchain/EU Compliance/ipr-che.notes: First IPR (Instant Payments Regulation, Reg (EU) 2024/886) concept page added to the wiki, then immediately double-confirmed against the IPR.html verification artefact within the same session (v1.0 → v1.1). Seventh EU compliance checklist ingest in the 2026-05-24–25 cluster (after MiCA v2.3, DORA v1.2, MiFID II v1.1, DLT Pilot v1.2, GDPR v1.1, TFR v1.0). IPR is structurally distinct from every prior compliance ingest: it is the FIRST AMENDING-ACT-ONLY regime added to the wiki. Every prior ingest dealt with a standalone primary act; IPR Reg (EU) 2024/886 is a surgical amender of four existing acts (SEPA Reg 260/2012, Cross-Border Payments Reg 2021/1230, PSD2 Dir 2015/2366, SFD Dir 98/26/EC). Concept page accordingly leads with 'what-it-does-in-one-sentence' + 'the four acts it amends' table BEFORE the obligation summary — readers need to know the substantive obligations live in the amended SEPA Reg Articles 5a–5d, not in the IPR itself. NO new source page created — [[EU-PSD3-IPR-FiDA-AIAct-2026]] external-research source page (created 2026-04-16) already covers the IPR + PSD3 + FiDA + AI Act + eIDAS 2.0 2025–2026 cluster; concept page references it in Sources. Same pattern as the dlt-pilot-checklist ingest (filed against existing autoresearch source page, no new source page). Verification methodology: seventh use this week of the local-EUR-Lex-HTML pattern. User had previously saved IPR.html (187KB — smallest of the standing verification corpus because the IPR text is only ~6 Articles long). Python HTMLParser strip yielded 760-line plain text in /tmp; all article-level citations cross-checked verbatim: Art 1(1) six new definitions (instant credit transfer / payment initiation channel / PISP / name of the payee / targeted financial restrictive measure / LEI) / 5a(1)–(8) full instant-CT obligation including 10-second budget + reachability symmetry + 24/7 + phased deadlines by zone × entity type / 5b(1)–(3) charge parity + VoP free / 5c(1)(a)–(d) four VoP scenarios verbatim including legal-person LEI/EUI/fiscal-number alternative identifier / 5c(8) Art 88 PSD2 liability shield contingent on VoP performance + refund-without-delay + PISP/payee-PSP indemnity chain / 5d(1)–(3) daily PSU sanctions screening + prohibition on transaction-level screening during SCT Inst execution / 11(1b) penalty ceilings ≥10% consolidated turnover + ≥€5M / 11(1c) planned-maintenance carve-out / 15(3)–(6) reporting cycle including first PSP report 9 Apr 2025 + Commission obstacles report 9 Apr 2027 + Commission Art 5b/5d effectiveness report 9 Oct 2028 / 16(9) newly-euro-adopting Member State transition rule / 2(1) IPR cross-border charge alignment / 3(1)–(3) PSD2 amendments — Art 10 tightened safeguarding + Art 35 group-only exemption + indirect-access fairness duty + new Art 35a three mandatory artefacts (safeguarding description with full sub-items, governance + internal controls + ICT controls description with explicit DORA Arts 6 and 7 cross-reference, winding-up plan; 9 governance sub-items (a)–(i) verbatim) / 4 SFD amendments adding PIs and EMIs to 'institution' definition with Art 32/33 PSD2 exemption carve-outs / 5 transposition deadline 9 Apr 2025 / 6 entry into force 8 Apr 2024. Key insights: (1) 'IPR' is ambiguous — user asked for 'checklist for IPR' with IPR.html source, which could read as Intellectual Property Rights; confirmed via file inspection that source is Reg (EU) 2024/886 Instant Payments Regulation; pre-emptively flagged ambiguity to user before mirroring; user confirmed by saying 'mirror'. (2) Article 5d daily-screening deadline 9 Jan 2025 is the earliest binding IPR obligation across every zone × entity type — euro CI, non-euro CI, euro PI/EMI, non-euro PI/EMI all hit 9 Jan 2025, not the 9 Oct 2025 deadline most market commentary focuses on; as of today (2026-05-25), any PSP not already daily-screening is non-compliant. (3) Article 5d is the most-misread provision in the Regulation — the prohibition on transaction-level sanctions screening during SCT Inst execution is narrowly scoped to Article-215-TFEU asset freezes / funds-availability prohibitions only, NOT to AML/CFT transaction monitoring or other restrictive-measure types; PSPs commonly read it as 'you can stop sanctions screening on SCT Inst', which is wrong — the burden is reallocated (per-transaction → daily PSU-level), not removed. (4) PSD2 Art 35a is the IPR's deliverable-shaped service surface — three required artefacts (safeguarding description with sub-items, governance + internal controls + ICT controls description with explicit DORA Arts 6 and 7 cross-reference, winding-up plan) are the price of admission for PI/EMI prospects seeking direct SFD-designated-system access (TIPS / RT1 / national instant rails); fit with InnBlockchain's existing DORA cluster work makes this an Article 35a build as DORA artefact extension rather than separate workstream. (5) IPR vs MiCA boundary mirrors MiFID II vs MiCA boundary — pure CASPs are NOT in IPR scope; IPR binds PSPs offering euro credit transfers, and CASP fiat on/off-ramp typically rides on a partner PSP, in which case IPR follows the partner PSP not the CASP; same shape as TFR dual-classification logic for EMTs. (6) Methodology pattern is now seven-for-seven (mica.html / dora.html / dlt.html / mifid2.html + mifid3-791.html / gdpr.html / amla.html / tfr.html / IPR.html) — workflow is identical: user saves EUR-Lex HTML locally → Python HTMLParser strip to /tmp → systematic Article-by-Article confirmation → corrections folded back into source pre-final-ingest → verification artefact registered in concept-page frontmatter + manifest. .raw/InnBlockchain/EU Compliance/tfr-che.notes: First TFR (Travel Rule, Reg (EU) 2023/1113) concept page added to the wiki. Sixth EU compliance checklist ingest in the 2026-05-24–25 cluster (after MiCA v2.3, DORA v1.2, MiFID II v1.1, DLT Pilot v1.2, GDPR v1.1). TFR is structurally distinct from prior ingests in three ways: (1) it is a RECAST regulation (replacing 2015/847), not a fresh regime — Chapter II PSP obligations carry forward the prior text materially unchanged, the novelty lives in Chapter III (CASP, Arts 14–22); (2) it is the FIRST 'data accompaniment' regulation in the cluster — every prior EU compliance regulation (MiCA / DORA / MiFID II / DLT Pilot / GDPR / AMLR) governs authorisation, conduct, or processing scope; TFR uniquely governs what information must travel WITH a transfer; (3) it is the FIRST EU regulation that explicitly treats EMTs as crypto-assets not funds (Art 2 final indent) — EMI issuers tokenizing on DLT face dual PSP/CASP classification under a single regulation. New source page [[InnBlockchain-TFR-Checklist]] created (no prior autoresearch source page existed for TFR — same fresh-source-page pattern as the GDPR ingest two days prior). Concept page [[TFR-Regulation]] follows the established strategic/positioning layer format (matching MiCA-Regulation.md / DORA-Regulation.md / EU-AML-AMLA.md) — explicitly NOT a *-Mechanics practitioner companion (gap flagged for future extraction from EBA Travel Rule guidelines + IVMS101 protocol docs + counterparty-failure escalation playbook). Key insights flagged in source/concept pages: (1) Art 14(4) 'in advance of, simultaneously, or concurrently with' + Art 14(8) block-then-broadcast together create a hard architectural rule — off-chain Travel Rule message MUST be delivered before/with the on-chain broadcast, AND broadcast must be blocked until full Art 14 compliance is established; (2) NO de minimis for crypto transfers (Recital 30) — distinct from the €1,000 funds threshold; (3) self-hosted address €1,000 ownership-verification trigger (Arts 14(5), 16(2)) is a FLOOR not a ceiling; (4) AMLD5 Art 19b correspondent CASP regime imported by Art 38(4) is the second-largest practical compliance lift after the Travel Rule itself; (5) EMTs are crypto-assets under TFR not funds — Art 2 final indent — most commonly-missed scoping point for EMI issuers; (6) GDPR–TFR collision: Union → third-country CASP transfers where GDPR Chapter V cannot be satisfied → operational answer is to refuse to execute, per EBA/EDPB guidelines mandated under Art 25(4); (7) sixth EU compliance verification pass this week — methodology is now six-for-six (MiCA, DLT Pilot, DORA, MiFID II, GDPR, TFR — all locally-saved EUR-Lex HTML → HTMLParser → verbatim Article-by-Article confirmation → corrections folded into source pre-ingest where applicable).
